_meta.notes: Second town up the northern road, just below Highfield itself. Apothecary town. The cloudtea bushes grow most densely here and the locals have learned to dry the leaves on bone-frame racks in the steady cold wind. Coldspoon also extracts a clear blue tincture from cloudtea ('cloud-bitters') that orcish Hush-readers buy by the case.

tagline: An apothecary town in the cold wind. The cloudtea grows wild here.
ambientPool: Drying racks line the south slope, bone-frames hung with cloudtea leaves.
An apothecary measures a tincture by drops with a polished bone spoon.
Two mule-guides from Three Notches deliver a fresh load of cider casks to be aged at altitude.
The wind here is cold enough that the locals all wear at least three layers; foreigners are obvious.

rooms.summary: South-facing slope covered in bone-frame drying racks. The wind does most of the work; the apothecaries only turn the leaves twice a day. The smell is faintly bitter, faintly sweet, and stays in your hair afterward.
ambientPool: An apothecary's apprentice turns a frame of leaves with a wooden paddle and counts under their breath.
A flock of high-altitude songbirds passes; nobody pauses for them.
A leaf falls from a rack and is retrieved by a child who has been waiting for one.
The wind is cold even with the sun on your back.

exits.narrative: Into the open-fronted tincture room where the cloud-bitters are made.

rooms.summary: Open-fronted room — three walls and a roof, the south wall absent. Long benches of small jars catching drops from suspended cloth filters. The clear blue cloud-bitters condenses slowly; one master can produce a single full bottle in a day.
ambientPool: A master apothecary changes a filter without spilling a drop.
A delivery from the Long Indices is logged: six bottles ordered, six bottles ready.
An apprentice tastes a fresh batch on the back of their hand and nods, satisfied.
The light through the open south wall makes the bottles glow.

exits.narrative: Back out to the drying slope.
